assembly_instructions: Read MiniMax-M1 as a HYBRID-ATTENTION FACTORY. Tokens enter at the floor (X=0,Y=0) and climb the orange residual pillar through 80 transformer blocks. The killer trick: 7 LIGHTNING ATTENTION blocks (linear, O(N)) for every 1 SOFTMAX ATTENTION block. Lightning blocks are coloured pale-amber, softmax blocks are deep-crimson — count them. Lightning attention has NO KV cache (it carries a fixed-size linear-attention state instead, the cyan torus to the left at X=-2.5); softmax attention carries the conventional GQA KV cache (amber column at X=-3.5). To the right at X=6 sits the per-layer MoE FFN (1 shared + 32 routed experts, top-2). Above the stack at X=2/Y=9 is the CISPO RL trainer (red sphere) — MiniMax-M1's reasoning post-training algorithm (clipped sequence-level importance sampling). The translucent slab at X=8 marks the parallelism plan and the 41-day RL training run. Click any part for FLOPs, memory, parallelism class, scratch-build pseudocode, the dominant bottleneck, and improvement ideas.

info.summary: 456B-parameter Mixture-of-Experts reasoning model from MiniMax (45.9B activated per token). The signature feature is a HYBRID attention stack: 7 LIGHTNING-attention layers (linear-time, no KV cache) for every 1 SOFTMAX-attention layer (standard GQA with KV cache). Built on MiniMax-Text-01, then post-trained with the new CISPO RL algorithm over 41 days on H800s. 1 M-token native input context, 80 K output budget for very long chains-of-thought.
info.description:
MiniMax-M1 is the reasoning-extension of MiniMax-Text-01. The two carry the same architecture: 80 transformer layers, hidden 6144, 32 routed experts + 1 shared with top-2 routing, but the attention is HYBRID — lightning-attention (linear) layers replace standard softmax attention 7-out-of-8 times. Lightning attention is the right-product / kernel form of linear attention: it maintains a fixed-size hidden state S ∈ R^{d_k × d_v} that is updated per token, so the per-token cost is O(d_k · d_v) regardless of context. Only the 1-in-8 softmax layers see the full O(T) attention, and they use GQA. The result: at 1M context the attention compute and KV memory are dominated by the 10 softmax layers, not the 70 lightning layers — which makes 1M context economical.

M1 specifically adds reasoning via CISPO (Clipped IS-weight Policy Optimisation): a sequence-level importance-sampling RL with token-level clipping designed to handle very long rollouts (up to 80K output tokens) without the variance blow-up that kills naïve PPO/GRPO at this length.

Design rationale, in one line each:
• Lightning attention: linear-time attention with fixed-size state — 1M context becomes affordable.
• 7:1 hybrid: pure linear attention loses associative recall; the 1-in-8 softmax layer restores it.
• Sparse MoE (32 experts top-2): cheap per-token capacity at large total scale.
• CISPO RL over 41 days: the training time itself is a feature — long-horizon reasoning needs many rollout steps.

facts.label: lightning kernel
facts.value: Right-product linear attention, ReLU feature map

facts.label: RL reward sources
facts.value: Math, code, agentic tool-use, software engineering

comparisons.delta: MiniMax-Text-01 introduced the 7:1 lightning/softmax hybrid and the 32-expert MoE. M1 keeps the architecture EXACTLY but adds a long, stable RL-for-reasoning phase using CISPO. M1-80k is the same base weights with a much larger output budget (80K tokens vs 8K) — the architecture had to support 1M input from day one for this to work.

abab-class models were standard MoE Transformers with full softmax attention. MiniMax-Text-01 swapped 7-out-of-8 attention layers for lightning-attention linear-time layers, which is the single biggest change in MiniMax's history. M1 inherits all of that and only changes the post-training pipeline.

Both are MoE reasoning models. DeepSeek-V3/R1 use ALL softmax attention (MLA-compressed) — M1 uses 7/8 LINEAR attention. At 1M context, M1's KV footprint is ~10× smaller than V3's because 70 of 80 layers carry no KV cache at all. M1 is also smaller per token (45.9B activated vs 37B — comparable). DeepSeek-R1 trained with GRPO; M1 trained with CISPO designed for longer rollouts.

Public consensus says GPT-4 and Claude families use full softmax attention (likely with FlashAttention + GQA) and dense or coarse-MoE FFNs. M1 sits at a different point: it gives up some short-context attention quality in exchange for native 1M-token input being affordable. For long-document reasoning that trade is worth it; for short interactive chat, full-softmax peers retain a quality edge.

parts.name: Input Tokens (V≈200K)
parts.role: Token-ID stream. Tokeniser is MiniMax's BPE (~200K vocab).

parts.name: Embedding (V≈200K, d=6144)
parts.role: Tied embedding / LM head. ~1.23B params (~2.46 GiB BF16).

parts.name: Residual Stream (d=6144, 80 layers)
parts.role: Pre-norm RMSNorm residual carry through all 80 layers.

parts.name: × 80 Layers (7 LIGHTNING : 1 SOFTMAX)
parts.role: Marker for the 80-layer depth: 70 lightning + 10 softmax. Pattern: L L L L L L L S repeating 10 times.

parts.name: Block 1 — LIGHTNING (linear)
parts.role: Lightning-attention block. O(d_k·d_v) per token, NO KV cache.

parts.name: Block 2 — LIGHTNING
parts.role: Lightning block 2.

parts.name: Block 3 — LIGHTNING
parts.role: Lightning block 3.

parts.name: Block 4 — LIGHTNING
parts.role: Lightning block 4.

parts.name: Block 8 — SOFTMAX (GQA, 1M ctx)
parts.role: SOFTMAX attention block — full causal softmax over the 1M-token context. Carries the GQA KV cache. Restores associative recall that lightning attention loses.

parts.name: Block 9 — LIGHTNING
parts.role: Lightning block 9. Pattern resumes.

parts.name: Block 10 — LIGHTNING
parts.role: Lightning block 10.

parts.name: Block 16 — SOFTMAX
parts.role: Second softmax block. (Layers 11–15 lightning, elided.)

parts.name: Block 80 — SOFTMAX (final)
parts.role: Final softmax block before the LM head.

parts.name: Lightning · Pre-RMSNorm
parts.role: Pre-norm before the lightning sublayer.

parts.name: Lightning · QKV Proj (+ feature map ϕ)
parts.role: Project x to Q, K, V then apply a non-negative feature map ϕ (e.g. ReLU or 1+ELU) so that the linear-attention kernel ϕ(Q)·ϕ(K)^T is positive.
parts.algorithm:
Q = ϕ(x @ W_Q)
K = ϕ(x @ W_K)
V = x @ W_V

parts.name: Lightning Attention Kernel (right-product)
parts.role: The core trick: maintain a recurrent state S ∈ R^{d_k × d_v} and a normaliser z ∈ R^{d_k}. Each token updates S += K_t ⊗ V_t and outputs Q_t @ S / (Q_t @ z). Per-token cost is O(d_k · d_v) — INDEPENDENT of context length.
parts.flops_per_token: ≈ 4·d_k·d_v ≈ 200 KFLOP — basically free vs softmax attention
parts.memory_footprint: S: d_k × d_v × dtype per-layer-per-sequence = ~1 MB / sequence / layer
parts.algorithm:
# during decode (per token):
S += outer(K_t, V_t)        # O(d_k · d_v)
z += K_t                    # O(d_k)
out_t = (Q_t @ S) / (Q_t @ z + eps)

# during prefill (chunked):
# split into chunks, parallel intra-chunk softmax-style,
# pass S, z forward across chunks.
parts.bottleneck: Sequential along time during decode — no across-token parallelism. Chunked prefill recovers parallelism for long inputs.
improvement_ideas: Chunk-parallel prefill (already used) to hide the recurrence.
Replace ReLU feature map with a learnt gating (Mamba / GLA-style) for better recall.
Hybridise with sliding-window per-layer for cheap local attention.

parts.name: Lightning State (S, z) — fixed size
parts.role: Per-sequence per-layer recurrent state S ∈ R^{d_k × d_v}. Replaces the KV cache for lightning layers. Crucially, its SIZE IS INDEPENDENT of context length — that is what makes 1M-token context economical.
parts.memory_footprint: ≈ 1–2 MB per sequence per layer (independent of T!) — vs ~2 KB / token / layer for GQA KV.
parts.algorithm:
# state stays in HBM (or registers during chunk compute)
S, z = zeros(d_k, d_v), zeros(d_k)
improvement_ideas: Quantise S to FP8 between chunks.
Allow per-head state expansion (Mamba-like) for richer memory.

parts.name: Lightning · O-Projection
parts.role: Output projection from d_v back to d.

parts.name: Softmax · QKV Proj (GQA 64/8)
parts.role: QKV projection for the 1-in-8 softmax layers. 64 query heads share 8 KV heads.
parts.algorithm:
Q = x @ W_Q  # 64 heads
K = x @ W_K  # 8 heads (GQA)
V = x @ W_V  # 8 heads
Q, K = apply_rope(Q, K, pos)

parts.name: Softmax Attention (FlashAttention-2)
parts.role: Standard causal softmax attention over the full 1M-token context. Only runs in 10 of 80 layers.
parts.bottleneck: These 10 layers dominate attention compute and KV memory at 1M context.
improvement_ideas: Ring-attention sharding across sequence parallelism for these 10 layers.
Sparse top-k attention for these layers (DeepSeek-V4 style).

parts.name: Softmax · O-Projection
parts.role: Output projection back to d.

parts.name: Softmax KV Cache (10 layers only)
parts.role: Standard GQA KV cache for the 10 softmax layers. Lightning layers carry no cache. Net result at 1M ctx: ~10× less KV memory than an all-softmax model.
parts.memory_footprint: ≈ 2 × 8 × 128 × FP16 = 4 KB / token / softmax-layer; 10 layers × 1M ctx ≈ 38 GiB / sequence

parts.name: Top-2 Router (softmax, capacity)
parts.role: Per-token softmax router over 32 experts, top-2. Token-choice with capacity factor; tokens above capacity fall through to the shared expert.
parts.algorithm:
scores = softmax(x @ W_router)
topk_idx, topk_w = topk(scores, 2)
# capacity gate:
if over_capacity(topk_idx[0]): drop_to_shared(token)

parts.name: Routed Experts (32, top-2, d_ff=9216)
parts.role: Per-MoE-layer bank of 32 SwiGLU experts (d_ff=9216 — much wider than DeepSeek's fine-grained 3072).
improvement_ideas: Group GEMM to fuse the two expert calls.
FP4 weight-only quantisation on the expert GEMMs.

parts.name: Shared Expert (×1)
parts.role: Always-on expert that absorbs dropped tokens and common computation.

parts.name: Expert All-to-All
parts.role: NCCL all-to-all dispatch + combine. Smaller than DeepSeek-V4 because top-2 (not top-8).
parts.algorithm:
grouped = bucket_by_expert(x, topk_idx)
recv    = nccl.all_to_all(grouped)
# expert FFN ↓
out_grp = bucket_by_origin(expert_out)
out     = nccl.all_to_all(out_grp)
improvement_ideas: Fuse with attention O-proj of the previous layer.

parts.name: LM Head (tied)
parts.role: Tied projection back to ~200K vocab.

parts.name: LM Loss (cross-entropy)
parts.role: Pre-training next-token loss.

parts.name: CISPO RL Trainer (clipped IS, 80K rollouts)
parts.role: Clipped IS-weight Policy Optimisation. Sequence-level importance ratio with token-level clipping bounds variance on very long (up to 80K-token) reasoning rollouts. The single biggest reason M1 can be RL-trained for 41 days without diverging.
parts.algorithm:
for batch in rollouts:
    rho = pi_new(τ) / pi_old(τ)            # sequence-level IS
    rho = clip(rho, 1-ε_low, 1+ε_high)
    A   = reward_model(τ) - baseline
    loss = -mean( clip_token(rho) * A )    # token-level clip
    policy.update(loss)
improvement_ideas: Adaptive clipping ε from rollout-length statistics.
Off-policy replay across multiple training rounds.

parts.name: Reward Models (math / code / agent / SWE)
parts.role: Domain-specific reward sources used during CISPO RL: verifiable math/code rewards + learned agentic and SWE-bench rewards.

parts.name: AdamW (β1=0.9, β2=0.95)
parts.role: Standard AdamW for both pre-training and RL. ZeRO-1 sharding across DP.

parts.name: TP × EP × PP × DP (training plan)
parts.role: Multi-axis parallelism plan. Lightning layers parallelise differently from softmax: lightning needs a sequence-parallel chunked prefill + state-passing across PP boundaries; softmax uses standard TP.
improvement_ideas: Pipeline lightning state with the activation transfer at PP boundaries.

parts.name: Pre-training Corpus + Long-CoT RL (41 days)
parts.role: Pre-training data plate inherited from MiniMax-Text-01. M1 adds a 41-day CISPO RL phase on math/code/agent/SWE rollouts with long chains-of-thought.
